subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students often exclude coal seams thinking only geological formations count; all three are valid geological carbon sinks.
elimination_hint: All three—coal seams, depleted reservoirs, and deep saline formations—are established CCS sites; eliminate any option that excludes one.
factual_hooks: Carbon sequestration uses geological formations to store CO2
Subterranean saline formations have the largest storage capacity globally
Depleted oil/gas reservoirs have proven sealing capacity
contrast_with: Carbon offsetting vs carbon sequestration — sequestration is physical storage, offsetting is compensatory
contemporary_relevance: India's net-zero 2070 commitment and National Action Plan on Climate Change make CCS increasingly relevant.

subject: History
trap_factor: Sautrantika and Sammitiya are actually sects of Buddhism, not Jainism—the question exploits confusion between Buddhist sub-schools and Jain sects.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is clearly wrong (these are Buddhist sects); Statement 2 about Sarvastivadin's philosophy of eternal dharmas is correct, making B the answer.
factual_hooks: Sautrantika and Sammitiya are Hinayana Buddhist schools, not Jain sects
Sarvastivadin doctrine holds that dharmas exist in past, present, and future
contrast_with: Hinayana sects vs Mahayana schools — different philosophical positions on dharma

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Jordan and Iraq are often confused as Mediterranean countries; Jordan borders the Red Sea/Gulf of Aqaba and Iraq is landlocked relative to the Mediterranean.
elimination_hint: Only Lebanon and Syria have Mediterranean coastlines among the four; eliminate Jordan (Red Sea) and Iraq (no Mediterranean coast).
factual_hooks: Lebanon and Syria are the only Mediterranean-bordering countries in this list
Jordan borders the Red Sea/Gulf of Aqaba, not Mediterranean
Iraq has no Mediterranean coastline
contrast_with: Mediterranean vs Red Sea vs Arabian Sea coastlines of Middle Eastern nations
contemporary_relevance: Geopolitical importance of Eastern Mediterranean for energy and trade routes.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement 1 is a common misconception—algae can be grown in ponds/bioreactors on land, not only in seas; this wrong statement traps those unfamiliar with algae cultivation.
elimination_hint: Eliminate statement 1 (algae biofuels don't require seas); statements 2 and 3 are genuine limitations, making B correct.
factual_hooks: Algae biofuels can be grown in land-based ponds and closed bioreactors
High capital costs and expertise requirements limit developing country adoption
Large-scale facilities may cause ecological disruption
contrast_with: First-gen (food crop) vs third-gen (algae) biofuels — land-use and ethical concerns differ
contemporary_relevance: India's National Biofuel Policy 2018 promotes advanced biofuels including algae-based options.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Many assume Fundamental Duties are legally enforceable like Fundamental Rights, but no specific legislative mechanism exists to enforce them; they are also moral duties, not correlative legal duties.
elimination_hint: Both statements are false—no enforcement mechanism exists and duties are moral not legal correlatives—so 'Neither 1 nor 2' (D) is correct.
factual_hooks: Fundamental Duties (Article 51A) have no enforcement mechanism in the Constitution
Fundamental Duties are moral obligations, not correlative to legal rights
contrast_with: Fundamental Rights (enforceable) vs Fundamental Duties (non-justiciable)
contemporary_relevance: Debate on making Fundamental Duties enforceable resurfaces periodically in judicial and legislative discourse.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: The route from Kohima (Nagaland) to Kottayam (Kerala) crosses many states; candidates miscalculate by forgetting Northeast connectivity or counting incorrectly.
elimination_hint: Nagaland → Assam → West Bengal → Odisha → Andhra Pradesh → Tamil Nadu → Kerala = 7 states including origin and destination.
factual_hooks: Kohima is in Nagaland; Kottayam is in Kerala
Road route must pass through Assam, West Bengal, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu
contrast_with: Air vs road distance — road requires transit through multiple states unlike air travel

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 2 has inverted figures—Centre has ~49% domestic liabilities, States ~21%—which traps candidates who remember numbers but confuse which government has which.
elimination_hint: Statement 2 is wrong (figures are reversed); Statements 1 and 3 are correct—FRBM recommended 60% (40%+20%) and Article 293 requires state consent from Centre for borrowing.
factual_hooks: FRBM Review Committee: 60% debt-to-GDP (40% Centre + 20% States)
Article 293(3): States need Centre consent to borrow if outstanding dues exist
contrast_with: Centre vs State borrowing limits under FRBM framework
contemporary_relevance: States' fiscal health and borrowing limits remain contentious post-COVID amid rising debt.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 2 (lottery basis) and 3 (self-sufficient) are both wrong—coal is auctioned since SC 2014 ruling and India still imports coking coal—but candidates may accept these plausible-sounding claims.
elimination_hint: Only Statement 1 is correct (coal nationalized under Indira Gandhi via Coal Mines Nationalization Acts 1971-73); both 2 and 3 are factually incorrect.
factual_hooks: Coal nationalization: 1971 (coking coal) and 1973 (all coal) under Indira Gandhi
Post-2014 SC ruling: coal blocks allocated via competitive auction, not lottery
India still imports metallurgical/coking coal
contrast_with: Nationalization era vs post-liberalization commercial mining policy
contemporary_relevance: Commercial coal mining opened to private sector in 2020, a major policy reversal.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statements 1 and 2 appear logical but are wrong—Ramsar doesn't mandate conservation of ALL wetlands, and the 2010 Rules were framed under the Environment Protection Act, not Ramsar.
elimination_hint: Only Statement 3 is correct (Rules cover catchment/drainage areas); Ramsar is non-binding on selection of specific sites, eliminating statements 1 and 2.
factual_hooks: Ramsar Convention is not mandatory for protecting ALL wetlands—nations choose Ramsar sites
Wetlands Rules 2010 based on Environment Protection Act 1986, not Ramsar directly
Rules include drainage/catchment areas of wetlands
contrast_with: Ramsar sites (internationally designated) vs all wetlands (nationally protected)
contemporary_relevance: India has the highest number of Ramsar sites in Asia as of 2023.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Both statements assert false limitations of solar power—solar can run both surface and submersible pumps, and both centrifugal and piston pumps, trapping those with incomplete knowledge.
elimination_hint: Solar power drives DC/AC motors of any pump type; neither statement is true, so 'Neither 1 nor 2' (D) is the answer.
factual_hooks: Solar pumps can power both surface and submersible pump types
Solar energy can drive centrifugal as well as piston pumps via electric motors
contrast_with: Diesel pump vs solar pump economics for irrigation
contemporary_relevance: PM-KUSUM scheme targets 3.5 million solar agricultural pumps by 2026.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Candidates might think only industrial sources emit nitrogen compounds; all three agricultural/livestock sources are genuine emitters of reactive nitrogen.
elimination_hint: All three statements are scientifically correct—agricultural soils emit N2O, cattle emit NH3, and poultry releases reactive nitrogen compounds; select D.
factual_hooks: Agricultural soils emit nitrous oxide (N2O) through microbial denitrification
Cattle release ammonia through urine and manure decomposition
Poultry industry is a significant source of reactive nitrogen pollution
contrast_with: Nitrogen fixation (beneficial) vs reactive nitrogen pollution (harmful)
contemporary_relevance: Reactive nitrogen management is increasingly important for India's air quality and water pollution goals.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The name suggests DST or MSDE as the nodal ministry, but Atal Innovation Mission is under NITI Aayog, not a line ministry.
elimination_hint: AIM was set up under NITI Aayog in 2016 as India's flagship innovation promotion platform; eliminate all ministry options.
factual_hooks: Atal Innovation Mission (AIM) is set up under NITI Aayog
AIM promotes Atal Tinkering Labs in schools and Atal Incubation Centres
contrast_with: AIM (NITI Aayog) vs Startup India (DPIIT) — different nodal agencies for innovation
contemporary_relevance: AIM has established 10,000+ Atal Tinkering Labs; relevant to India's science and technology governance.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students confuse Arctic and Antarctic effects on June 21—the Sun doesn't set at Arctic Circle (midnight sun) but it does set at Antarctic Circle (polar night).
elimination_hint: June 21 = Summer Solstice = midnight sun at Arctic Circle; Sun shines overhead at Tropic of Cancer (not Equator or Capricorn); select A.
factual_hooks: June 21 (Summer Solstice): Sun overhead at Tropic of Cancer
Arctic Circle experiences midnight sun (no sunset) on June 21
Antarctic Circle experiences polar night on June 21
contrast_with: June 21 effects at Arctic vs Antarctic Circle—opposite experiences

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 2 says Xavier died in Goa—he actually died on Shangchuan Island (China) in 1552, not Goa; his remains were later brought to Goa.
elimination_hint: Statement 2 is wrong (Xavier died in China, not Goa); Statements 1 and 3 are correct, making C the answer.
factual_hooks: St. Francis Xavier was a co-founder of the Jesuit Order (1540)
Xavier died on Shangchuan Island near China in 1552; remains enshrined at Bom Jesus Basilica, Goa
Feast of St. Francis Xavier celebrated on December 3 in Goa
contrast_with: Xavier's death in China vs remains preserved in Goa—a common source of confusion

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Increasing CRR/SLR actually reduces money multiplier by keeping more reserves idle; candidates confuse 'more reserves' with 'more money creation'.
elimination_hint: Money multiplier = 1/CRR; higher banking habit means less cash leakage → more deposits → higher multiplier; select C.
factual_hooks: Money multiplier = 1/reserve ratio; higher banking habit reduces cash leakage
Increasing CRR or SLR decreases the money multiplier
Higher banking penetration means more deposits cycle through the system
contrast_with: CRR (reduces multiplier) vs banking habit (increases multiplier)—opposite effects
contemporary_relevance: UPI and Jan Dhan accounts increasing banking habit, affecting money creation dynamics.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Statement 2 is wrong—Laureus Sportsman of the Year was won mostly by athletes from various sports, not predominantly Formula One; F1's Sebastian Vettel won it a few times.
elimination_hint: Tiger Woods won the inaugural award (2000); Roger Federer holds the record for most wins; F1 domination claim is false—making C correct.
factual_hooks: Tiger Woods won the first Laureus World Sports Award (2000)
Roger Federer holds the record for most Laureus Sportsman of the Year awards
The award is not dominated by Formula One

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 is wrong—nanoparticles occur naturally (sea spray, volcanic ash, forest fires); many candidates assume all nanoparticles are man-made.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is false (natural nanoparticles exist); Statements 2 and 3 are correct (cosmetics and environmental safety concerns)—select D.
factual_hooks: Natural nanoparticles exist: sea spray, mineral weathering, volcanic emissions
Zinc oxide and titanium dioxide nanoparticles used in sunscreens and cosmetics
Engineered nanoparticles may pose ecotoxicological risks
contrast_with: Engineered nanoparticles vs naturally occurring nanoparticles
contemporary_relevance: India's National Nanotechnology Initiative and regulation of nanomaterials in cosmetics is ongoing.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Cabo Delgado is in Mozambique (not Spain) and Catalonia is in Spain (not Italy)—pairs 3 and 4 are swapped, trapping those who know one but not the other.
elimination_hint: Only pairs 1 (Anatolia-Turkey) and 2 (Amhara-Ethiopia) are correctly matched; Cabo Delgado is in Mozambique and Catalonia is in Spain—select B.
factual_hooks: Anatolia is the Asian part of Turkey (Anatolian Peninsula)
Amhara is a region in Ethiopia—site of conflict in 2020s
Cabo Delgado is in northern Mozambique, not Spain
Catalonia is a region in northeastern Spain, not Italy
contrast_with: Cabo Delgado (Mozambique) vs Canary Islands (Spain)—different regions in news
contemporary_relevance: Amhara and Cabo Delgado have been conflict zones in 2020-23, frequently appearing in current affairs.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement II is wrong—carbon markets transfer resources from polluters (private sector) to emission reducers (often also private), not to the State; candidates confuse carbon tax with carbon markets.
elimination_hint: Statement I is correct; Statement II is wrong (carbon markets are private-to-private, not private-to-state)—but the question says both are correct, making B the answer if II doesn't explain I.
factual_hooks: Carbon markets are seen as cost-effective tools for climate mitigation via cap-and-trade
Carbon markets transfer money from high-emitters to low-emitters—both typically private entities
Carbon markets are distinct from carbon taxes which do transfer to government
contrast_with: Carbon markets (private transfer) vs carbon tax (public revenue)
contemporary_relevance: India launched its Carbon Credit Trading Scheme in 2023 under the Energy Conservation Act.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Sterilization involves neutralizing foreign exchange inflows to prevent money supply expansion—students often confuse OMOs with routine RBI functions like debt management.
elimination_hint: Sterilization = absorbing excess liquidity from forex inflows via Open Market Operations (selling government securities); select A.
factual_hooks: Sterilization: RBI sells government securities (OMO) to mop up excess liquidity from forex inflows
OMOs are the primary sterilization tool used by RBI
Sterilization prevents exchange rate appreciation from inflating money supply
contrast_with: Sterilized vs non-sterilized forex intervention—former neutralizes money supply impact
contemporary_relevance: RBI regularly uses OMOs for liquidity management and sterilization of forex flows.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Gersoppa (Jog) Falls is on the Sharavathi River, not Netravati; and Dhuandhar is in Jabalpur (Madhya Pradesh), not Malwa plateau—two errors in three rows.
elimination_hint: Only row 2 (Hundru Falls on Subarnarekha in Chota Nagpur) is correct; Dhuandhar is on Narmada but in Jabalpur/Bhedaghat, not Malwa; Gersoppa is on Sharavathi—select A (only one correct).
factual_hooks: Hundru Falls: Subarnarekha River, Jharkhand (Chota Nagpur)
Dhuandhar Falls: Narmada River near Jabalpur/Bhedaghat, NOT Malwa
Gersoppa/Jog Falls: Sharavathi River, not Netravati
contrast_with: Sharavathi (Gersoppa) vs Netravati—two different Western Ghats rivers

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Parasitoid species are found only among insects with complex life cycles; candidates often include centipedes (not parasitoids) or exclude lesser-known groups like carabid beetles.
elimination_hint: Parasitoids found in: wasps (most common), flies (tachinid flies), and carabid beetles—three of the five; centipedes and termites are not parasitoids; select B.
factual_hooks: Parasitoid wasps are the most diverse parasitoid group
Tachinid flies are major insect parasitoids
Carabid beetles include some parasitoid species
Centipedes and termites are not parasitoids
contrast_with: Parasitoid (kills host) vs parasite (does not immediately kill host)
contemporary_relevance: Parasitoid wasps increasingly used in IPM (integrated pest management) in agriculture.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Lake Tanganyika appears to be near the equator but lies south of it; Tonle Sap is in Cambodia far from equator; Patos Lagoon is in Brazil, also south of equator.
elimination_hint: None of the three water bodies is crossed by the equator; Lake Tanganyika lies ~3-9°S, Tonle Sap ~12°N, Patos Lagoon ~31°S—select D (None).
factual_hooks: Lake Tanganyika lies between 3°S and 9°S—south of equator
Tonle Sap Lake (Cambodia) lies around 12-13°N
Patos Lagoon (Brazil) at approximately 31°S
contrast_with: Lake Victoria (equator crosses it) vs Lake Tanganyika (south of equator)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may be uncertain about whether Andhra Pradesh (not listed) or Telangana is correct; the question uses 2022-23 data where Telangana is indeed a major producer.
elimination_hint: All three statements are correct for 2022-23: India leads global turmeric production/export, 30+ varieties grown, and Maharashtra/Telangana/Karnataka/Tamil Nadu are the major states—select D.
factual_hooks: India produces ~80% of world's turmeric and is the largest exporter
Over 30 varieties of turmeric cultivated in India
Maharashtra, Telangana, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu are top producing states
contrast_with: Turmeric (Curcuma longa) vs ginger—both tropical rhizome crops with similar geography
contemporary_relevance: Turmeric's medicinal properties boosted demand post-COVID; India's turmeric exports reached record highs.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement I appears true as the Speaker decides defection cases but actually the Constitution says Speaker's decision is final—not President's; Statement II seems unlikely but is actually true—the Constitution uses 'political party' implicitly but doesn't define it in the body.
elimination_hint: Statement I is wrong (it's the Speaker/Chairman, not President, who decides per 10th Schedule Para 6); Statement II is also arguably wrong as 'political party' does appear in 10th Schedule—select D.
factual_hooks: 10th Schedule Para 6: Speaker/Chairman (not President) decides disqualification under anti-defection law
The term 'political party' appears in the 10th Schedule though not in the main body of Constitution
contrast_with: Speaker's quasi-judicial role in defection vs President's role in Governor appointment
contemporary_relevance: Anti-defection law under scrutiny after Maharashtra political crisis (2022) and Supreme Court rulings.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement II is wrong—the US and UK did NOT sign the Paris AI Action Summit's inclusive AI declaration; they declined to sign, citing sovereignty concerns.
elimination_hint: Statement I is correct (India co-chaired; builds on Bletchley 2023 and Seoul 2024); Statement II is wrong (US and UK did not sign)—select A.
factual_hooks: AI Action Summit held in Paris (Grand Palais), February 2025, co-chaired by France and India
Builds on Bletchley Park (2023) and Seoul (2024) AI Safety Summits
US and UK declined to sign the inclusive AI declaration at Paris 2025
contrast_with: AI safety governance (Bletchley focus) vs inclusive AI (Paris focus)—different emphases
contemporary_relevance: AI governance is a top geopolitical issue; India's co-chairmanship signals its global digital ambitions.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Abolition of untouchability (Article 17) and minority protection (Article 29-30) are under different fundamental rights categories—not under Right against Exploitation.
elimination_hint: Right against Exploitation (Articles 23-24) covers only: traffic/forced labour and child labour in factories/mines; select C (1 and 4 only).
factual_hooks: Article 23: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
Article 24: Prohibition of child labour in factories, mines, etc.
Untouchability abolition is Article 17 (Right to Equality), not Right against Exploitation
contrast_with: Right against Exploitation (Arts 23-24) vs Right to Equality (Arts 14-18)
contemporary_relevance: Human trafficking and bonded labour remain current policy concerns covered under Article 23.

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Clausewitz's dictum links war to political objectives
Contemporary geopolitics involves hybrid warfare, proxy conflicts, and economic coercion
contrast_with: Hard power (military) vs soft power (diplomacy) as tools of statecraft
mains_themes: relevance of Clausewitz in modern context
mains_intro: Clausewitz's assertion that war is diplomacy by other means reveals the deeply political nature of armed conflict, a thesis whose validity must be tested against contemporary geopolitical realities.
mains_body_points: Clausewitz argued war is never an end in itself but a rational instrument of state policy
Contemporary validation: Russia-Ukraine war shows military means serving geopolitical objectives (NATO expansion deterrence)
Limitations: Non-state actors, terrorism, and nuclear deterrence complicate rational-actor model
Hybrid warfare blurs the diplomacy-war boundary further
Ethical critique: framing war as 'diplomacy' risks normalizing violence as a legitimate policy tool
mains_conclusion: While Clausewitz's insight remains analytically powerful, ethical governance demands prioritizing dialogue over armed coercion in resolving contemporary geopolitical disputes.
contemporary_relevance: Russia-Ukraine conflict, Indo-Pacific tensions, and India's approach to border disputes all test this Clausewitzian framework.

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Durand Line (Afghanistan-Pakistan border) with McMahon Line (India-China/Tibet) or the Radcliffe Line (India-Pakistan Partition).
elimination_hint: Durand Line = Afghanistan-British India border (1893) agreed between Mortimer Durand and Afghan Amir; the map context of Afghan-Pakistan border points to A.
factual_hooks: Durand Line (1893): boundary between British India and Afghanistan, named after Mortimer Durand
McMahon Line: India-Tibet/China boundary (1914 Simla Convention)
Radcliffe Line: India-Pakistan partition boundary (1947)
contrast_with: Durand Line (Afghanistan border) vs McMahon Line (Tibet/China border)—commonly confused
contemporary_relevance: Pakistan-Afghanistan tensions over Durand Line remain a live geopolitical issue.

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Environmental clearances for border road projects involve national security vs ecological trade-offs
EIA processes can be expedited in strategic zones under national security provisions
contrast_with: Development imperative vs conservation ethic in border regions
mains_intro: Border infrastructure development in ecologically sensitive zones presents a fundamental ethical tension between national security imperatives and environmental stewardship.
mains_body_points: Strategic necessity: roads in border areas (Arunachal, Ladakh) are vital for troop mobilization and deterrence
Environmental cost: construction in Himalayas triggers landslides, disrupts biodiversity, and harms water sources
Ethical dilemma: compromising long-term ecological health for short-term security gains
Procedural ethics: EIA exemptions for border projects may bypass community rights
Balancing approach: phased development, green corridors, and technology-based monitoring
mains_conclusion: Ethical governance demands that border infrastructure decisions integrate transparent EIA, community consultation, and ecological safeguards without compromising legitimate national security needs.
contemporary_relevance: China's rapid border infrastructure vs India's delayed projects in Arunachal/Ladakh has intensified this debate.

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Values are deep-seated beliefs about what is important; ethics is the system of moral principles guiding behaviour
Professional competence without ethical grounding can lead to misuse of expertise
contrast_with: Values (what one holds important) vs Ethics (principles guiding action)—related but distinct
mains_intro: Values represent our fundamental beliefs about what matters, while ethics translates those beliefs into principled action—both are indispensable for a civil servant.
mains_body_points: Values: intrinsic beliefs (honesty, justice, compassion) formed through upbringing and experience
Ethics: systematic principles derived from values that guide decisions in specific contexts
Professional competence alone can be dangerous: technically skilled but ethically void officials cause harm (e.g., corruption, discrimination)
Historical examples: Nazi scientists show technical competence without ethical grounding is destructive
For civil servants: ethical competence ensures public trust, procedural fairness, and welfare-oriented outcomes
mains_conclusion: A civil servant must be both professionally competent and ethically grounded; competence without ethics is rudderless, while ethics without competence is ineffective.
contemporary_relevance: ARC II recommendations emphasize ethics training in civil services induction programmes.

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may guess six or eight hands associating Shiva with multiple arms; Nataraja specifically in Chola iconography always has four hands.
elimination_hint: Chola Nataraja bronze is a canonical image with four hands: upper right (damaru), upper left (flame), lower right (abhaya), lower left (pointing to raised foot)—select C.
factual_hooks: Chola Nataraja has four hands: damaru, fire, abhaya mudra, gajahasta
Nataraja represents Shiva as the cosmic dancer
Chola bronzes (9th-13th century) are finest examples of South Indian metal casting
contrast_with: Nataraja (four hands) vs Sadashiva (five faces, ten arms)—different Shaiva iconographic forms

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Variable cost reserve ratios sounds like it controls volume of credit selectively, but it is actually a quantitative (general) credit control tool, not selective.
elimination_hint: Selective credit controls target specific sectors; variable cost reserve ratios affect all credit uniformly—making D the odd one out; select D.
factual_hooks: Selective credit controls: margin requirements, rationing of credit, consumer credit regulation
CRR/SLR are quantitative (general) controls, not selective
Selective credit tools target specific assets like shares, commodities
contrast_with: Quantitative (CRR/SLR/Bank Rate) vs Qualitative/Selective (margin requirements, credit rationing)

subject: History
trap_factor: Yavanapriya literally means 'beloved of Yavanas (Greeks)' suggesting something luxurious; students might guess muslin or ivory, missing that pepper was the main trade commodity.
elimination_hint: Yavanapriya = pepper, the most prized Indian spice in Roman-Greek trade; muslin was known by other terms; select D.
factual_hooks: Yavanapriya ('beloved of Greeks') = black pepper in ancient Sanskrit texts
Pepper was India's most exported commodity to Rome and Greece
Sangam texts and Sanskrit sources reference Indian Ocean spice trade
contrast_with: Yavanapriya (pepper) vs Sirikol (muslin)—different luxury commodities in ancient trade

subject: History
trap_factor: The Dinesh Goswami Committee sounds like it could relate to insurgency or ethnic issues; it was specifically about electoral reforms in 1990.
elimination_hint: Dinesh Goswami Committee (1990) = electoral reforms, including anti-defection, electoral rolls, and election finance; select B.
factual_hooks: Dinesh Goswami Committee (1990): comprehensive recommendations on electoral reforms
Committee recommended depoliticization of election machinery and reform of anti-defection law
Not related to banking, insurgency, or Chakma issue
contrast_with: Dinesh Goswami (electoral reforms) vs Sarkaria Commission (centre-state relations)

subject: History
trap_factor: Gol Gumbaz in Bijapur has the second largest dome in the world; candidates often choose Jama Masjid or Sher Shah's tomb which are famous but smaller.
elimination_hint: Gol Gumbaz (Bijapur/Vijayapura) has one of the world's largest domes by diameter; select D.
factual_hooks: Gol Gumbaz, Bijapur: tomb of Muhammad Adil Shah with one of the largest domes globally
Built in 1656 CE
Famous for 'whispering gallery' inside the dome
contrast_with: Gol Gumbaz (Bijapur, largest dome) vs Taj Mahal (Agra, most famous dome)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may confuse Bank Rate with repo rate or lending rate; Bank Rate specifically refers to RBI rediscounting commercial paper/bills of exchange.
elimination_hint: Bank Rate = rate at which RBI rediscounts bills of exchange presented by commercial banks; select D.
factual_hooks: Bank Rate: rate at which RBI discounts bills of exchange and commercial paper
Distinguished from repo rate (collateralized short-term lending)
Bank Rate signals long-term monetary policy stance
contrast_with: Bank Rate (rediscounting, long-term) vs Repo Rate (collateralized, short-term)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Wheat and coffee might seem to have high international trade; rice actually has a small tradeable surplus globally despite being the largest food crop.
elimination_hint: Rice is mostly consumed domestically in producing countries (Asia); only a small fraction (~7%) of total production enters international trade—select A.
factual_hooks: Rice has the lowest international trade relative to production among major food crops
Asia consumes most of its rice domestically
Coffee, rubber, and cocoa have much higher trade-to-production ratios
contrast_with: Rice (subsistence crop, low trade) vs coffee/rubber (plantation crops, high trade share)

subject: History
trap_factor: Ashtapradhan is associated with Shivaji's Maratha state; candidates may confuse it with Gupta or Vijayanagar administrative systems.
elimination_hint: Ashtapradhan (council of eight ministers) was Shivaji's Maratha administrative innovation; select D.
factual_hooks: Ashtapradhan: Shivaji Maharaj's eight-minister council for Maratha administration
Peshwa was the most important among the eight ministers
Each minister had specific portfolios (Nyayadhish, Senapati, etc.)
contrast_with: Ashtapradhan (Marathas) vs Ashta Diggajas (Vijayanagar scholars)—similar names, different contexts

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may choose Jurassic Land Mass or Aryavarta; Gondwanaland is the established scientific term for the southern supercontinent.
elimination_hint: Gondwana Continent is the correct term for the ancient southern supercontinent that included India; select D.
factual_hooks: Gondwanaland: ancient supercontinent that broke apart ~180 million years ago
Included India, Africa, South America, Antarctica, Australia
India drifted north and collided with Eurasia to form Himalayas
contrast_with: Gondwana (southern) vs Laurasia (northern)—two components of Pangaea

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may limit UDHR reflection only to Fundamental Rights; the question tests knowledge that Preamble, DPSP, and Fundamental Duties all echo UDHR principles.
elimination_hint: Preamble (equality, dignity), DPSP (economic/social rights), and Fundamental Duties all reflect UDHR—all three are correct; select D.
factual_hooks: UDHR (1948) influenced India's Constitution adopted in 1950
Preamble's justice, equality, dignity echo UDHR Articles 1-2
DPSP Articles 39-51 mirror UDHR's social and economic rights
Fundamental Duties added by 42nd Amendment reflect UDHR's Article 29
related_concepts: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights
contrast_with: Fundamental Rights (legally enforceable) vs DPSP (non-justiciable) in relation to UDHR
contemporary_relevance: India's human rights record under UDHR framework is regularly reviewed at UN Human Rights Council UPR.

subject: History
trap_factor: The map of a southward military route through Deccan could suggest Rajendra Chola or Chandragupta; Malik Kafur's campaigns under Alauddin Khilji followed this exact route.
elimination_hint: Malik Kafur, general of Alauddin Khilji, conducted campaigns into Deccan and South India (1306-1311); select D.
factual_hooks: Malik Kafur was Alauddin Khilji's general who conquered Deccan and South India
Campaigns reached Madurai, Dvarasamudra, and Warangal
Route from Delhi southward through Deccan corresponds to Malik Kafur's military exploits
contrast_with: Malik Kafur (Delhi Sultanate southward) vs Rajendra Chola (Chola northward expansion)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may choose 'low pressure' or 'low temperature'; clouds float due to low density relative to surrounding air, not pressure or temperature directly.
elimination_hint: Clouds are water droplets/ice crystals less dense than surrounding air; buoyancy keeps them aloft—select D (low density).
factual_hooks: Clouds float due to low density—water droplets/ice crystals suspended in air are less dense than surrounding atmosphere
Cloud formation requires cooling of moist air to dew point
Updrafts also help maintain cloud height
contrast_with: Low density (correct cloud float reason) vs low pressure (incorrect)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Aravalli, Satpura, and Sahyadri all extend across multiple states; Ajanta Hills are confined to Maharashtra, making them the odd one out.
elimination_hint: Ajanta Hills (part of Satpura range system) are within Maharashtra only; Aravalli (Raj+Gujarat), Satpura (MP+Maha), Sahyadri (multiple states)—select C.
factual_hooks: Ajanta Hills are entirely within Maharashtra
Aravalli spreads across Rajasthan, Haryana, Gujarat, Delhi
Satpura Range extends across MP, Maharashtra, Chhattisgarh
Sahyadri (Western Ghats) spans multiple states from Gujarat to Kerala
contrast_with: Ajanta Hills (single state) vs Sahyadri (multi-state)—extent comparison

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: CSR under Companies Act 2013 mandates 2% profit spending for eligible companies
CSR can build brand reputation, employee morale, and community goodwill
contrast_with: Shareholder primacy model (Milton Friedman) vs stakeholder capitalism (CSR model)
mains_intro: Corporate Social Responsibility, far from being a cost, can function as a strategic investment that enhances both profitability and long-term sustainability.
mains_body_points: CSR builds brand equity and consumer loyalty, translating to revenue gains
Reduces regulatory and reputational risks, lowering long-term costs
Attracts and retains ethical talent, improving productivity
Enhances community relations, reducing conflict risks near operations
However: short-term profitability may be affected; CSR can be misused as 'greenwashing' without genuine commitment
mains_conclusion: When genuinely integrated into business strategy rather than treated as compliance, CSR creates shared value that makes companies more resilient, trusted, and profitable.
contemporary_relevance: India's mandatory CSR under Section 135 of Companies Act has channeled over ₹2 lakh crore since 2014.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: In 1994 India demonstrated capability to launch geostationary satellites for the first time via PSLV; students may confuse satellite building (earlier) with geostationary launch (1994).
elimination_hint: PSLV-D2 in 1994 demonstrated India's capability to launch geo-stationary satellites; India had built satellites earlier but the launch milestone was 1994—select D.
factual_hooks: PSLV's first successful launch: October 1994 (PSLV-D2)
India demonstrated geostationary satellite launch capability in 1994
INSAT series had previously used foreign launchers
contrast_with: LEO launch capability (earlier) vs geostationary launch (1994 milestone)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students might choose agriculture-related reasons; the correct answer relates to capital-intensive industrial investment pattern that failed to create employment.
elimination_hint: High capital/output ratio means more capital spent per unit of output with less employment generation; investment in capital-intensive industries kept people in agriculture—select A.
factual_hooks: India's planned industrialization was capital-intensive, limiting job creation
High capital-output ratio means low employment per unit of investment
Agricultural sector absorbed surplus labour due to lack of industrial jobs
contrast_with: Capital-intensive (high K/O ratio, low employment) vs labour-intensive (low K/O, high employment) industries

subject: History
trap_factor: Battle of Buxar involved Mir Qasim (not Mir Jafar); Battle of Chilianwala was against Sikhs (not Marathas); candidates mixing up these famous battles will choose wrong options.
elimination_hint: Battle of Wandiwash (1760) was a decisive British victory over the French (East India Company vs French); select B.
factual_hooks: Battle of Wandiwash (1760): French defeated by British (EIC) under Sir Eyre Coote
Battle of Buxar (1764): Mir Qasim, Nawab of Awadh + Mughal Emperor vs British
Battle of Chilianwala (1849): Second Anglo-Sikh War, not against Marathas
contrast_with: Battle of Wandiwash (Anglo-French) vs Battle of Plassey (Anglo-Nawab)

subject: History
trap_factor: Ambedkar is most associated with tribal/Dalit rights; Jyotiba Phule worked for Adivasis too—but Thakkar Bappa is specifically credited with first using the term 'Adivasi' for tribal communities.
elimination_hint: Amritlal Vithaldas Thakkar (Thakkar Bappa) first used 'Adivasi' to refer to tribal people and founded Bharatiya Adimjati Sevak Sangh; select B.
factual_hooks: Thakkar Bappa coined/popularized the term 'Adivasi' for tribal communities
Founded Bharatiya Adimjati Sevak Sangh for tribal welfare
Worked closely with Gandhi for tribal upliftment
contrast_with: Thakkar Bappa (Adivasi rights/welfare) vs B.R. Ambedkar (Dalit rights)

subject: History
trap_factor: Anuvrata means 'small vows' and candidates might confuse it with Buddhist vows; Anuvrata is specifically Jain—lesser vows for lay followers vs Mahavrata for monks.
elimination_hint: Anuvrata = small/minor vows prescribed for Jain lay followers (not monks); contrast with Mahavrata (great vows for monks); select C (Jainism).
factual_hooks: Anuvrata: five minor vows for Jain householders/laity
Mahavrata: five major vows for Jain monks (more strictly observed)
Anuvrata movement by Acharya Tulsi in 20th century revived these principles
contrast_with: Anuvrata (minor, for laity) vs Mahavrata (major, for monks) in Jainism

subject: History
trap_factor: Barrah Dacoity sounds like it might be from Punjab (known for revolutionary activities); it was actually in East Bengal (now Bangladesh) by the Dhaka Anushilan Samiti.
elimination_hint: Barrah Dacoity (1908) was conducted by the Dhaka Anushilan Samiti in East Bengal to fund revolutionary activities; select C.
factual_hooks: Barrah Dacoity (1908): first major revolutionary heist in Indian freedom movement
Conducted by Dhaka Anushilan Samiti in East Bengal
Collected funds to finance armed revolutionary activities
contrast_with: East Bengal revolutionary activities vs Punjab HSRA (Bhagat Singh era)—different organizations and periods

subject: Economy
trap_factor: High birth rate and low foreign aid seem intuitive causes; the real issue is structural—high capital-output ratio means investments yield less output and growth.
elimination_hint: High capital/output ratio means each unit of capital produces less output, resulting in low growth despite high savings; select D.
factual_hooks: High capital-output ratio = inefficient capital utilization = low growth per unit of investment
India's capital-intensive industrialization led to low employment and output growth
Harrod-Domar model: growth rate = savings rate / capital-output ratio
contrast_with: High savings but high ICOR → low growth vs low savings but low ICOR → moderate growth

subject: Economy
trap_factor: The sequence of establishment of Indian financial institutions is a niche factual question; IFCI (1948), ICICI (1955), UTI (1964), IDBI (1964) — UTI before IDBI makes this tricky.
elimination_hint: IFCI (1948) → ICICI (1955) → UTI (1964) → IDBI (1964) later that year; correct sequence is I, II, IV, III—select A.
factual_hooks: IFCI established 1948 (first development finance institution)
ICICI established 1955
UTI established February 1964; IDBI established June 1964

subject: History
trap_factor: Many confuse Sabarmati Ashram (starting point) with Dandi (destination); Gandhi started Civil Disobedience Movement from Sabarmati Ashram, not Dandi.
elimination_hint: Gandhi began the Dandi March (Civil Disobedience Movement) from Sabarmati Ashram on March 12, 1930; select C.
factual_hooks: Civil Disobedience Movement started from Sabarmati Ashram, Ahmedabad on March 12, 1930
Gandhi walked 241 miles to Dandi to break salt law on April 6, 1930
Dandi is the destination, Sabarmati is the starting point
contrast_with: Sabarmati (start) vs Dandi (end/salt law-breaking point)—commonly confused

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may think urea or ammonium sulphate are mixed fertilizers; a mixed fertilizer contains two or more of N, P, K—NPK is the classic example.
elimination_hint: NPK fertilizer contains nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium—the three primary macronutrients; it is the definitive mixed fertilizer; select D.
factual_hooks: NPK = Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium — the three primary plant macronutrients
Urea is a single-nutrient (N) fertilizer
Mixed fertilizers contain two or more primary nutrients
contrast_with: Single-nutrient (urea, DAP) vs mixed fertilizers (NPK)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Conflict of interest arises when personal interests could improperly influence professional judgment
Actual conflict: personal interest currently affecting decision; Potential conflict: could affect in future
contrast_with: Actual conflict (present, ongoing) vs potential conflict (future, contingent)
mains_intro: Conflict of interest occurs when a public servant's private interests could compromise—actually or potentially—their impartial discharge of official duties.
mains_body_points: Actual conflict: personal interest is currently and actively influencing a decision (e.g., official awarding contract to own company)
Potential conflict: interest exists that could influence future decisions (e.g., owning shares in a company one may regulate)
Perceived conflict: others reasonably believe interest may influence, even if it doesn't actually
Examples—actual: judge deciding case involving own investment; potential: minister with family land near proposed highway
Management: disclosure, recusal, divestiture are standard remedies
mains_conclusion: Managing both actual and potential conflicts of interest through transparency and institutional safeguards is essential to maintaining public trust in governance.
contemporary_relevance: Conflict of interest norms for ministers, bureaucrats, and regulators are a recurring governance reform issue.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Without seeing the actual map, this question requires elimination based on plausible geographic contexts; underwater relief contours (isobaths) are a standard oceanographic map feature.
elimination_hint: Lines along India's coastal region on maritime maps typically represent isobaths (underwater depth contours); select D.
factual_hooks: Isobaths/bathymetric contours show underwater relief on coastal maps
Coastal sovereignty extends to 12 nautical miles (territorial sea)
Continental shelf extends to 200nm (EEZ)
contrast_with: Isobaths (depth contours) vs isobars (pressure contours)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Zinc-carbon batteries are used in ordinary torches; nickel-cadmium (NiCd) are used in rechargeable devices like shavers—candidates often confuse primary and secondary batteries.
elimination_hint: Nickel-cadmium (NiCd) batteries are the rechargeable batteries used in shavers and portable devices; select A.
factual_hooks: Nickel-cadmium (NiCd): rechargeable secondary battery used in torches, shavers, power tools
Zinc-carbon: non-rechargeable primary battery
Lead-acid (lead peroxide + lead): car batteries
contrast_with: Rechargeable (NiCd, Li-ion) vs non-rechargeable (zinc-carbon, alkaline) batteries

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may choose superconductivity research which does use cryogenics; but rocket technology is the primary application of cryogenic engines in launch vehicles.
elimination_hint: Cryogenic engines burn liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen at extremely low temperatures; used in upper stages of rockets (GSLV); select C.
factual_hooks: Cryogenic engines: burn liquid H2 (−253°C) and liquid O2 (−183°C) as propellants in rockets
India's GSLV uses cryogenic upper stage (CE-20 engine)
Higher specific impulse makes cryogenic engines ideal for geostationary satellite launches
contrast_with: Cryogenic (high thrust, complex) vs solid fuel (simpler, lower performance) rocket engines
contemporary_relevance: India's cryogenic engine development was critical after technology denial; GSLV Mk III now uses indigenous CE-20.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Diesel and kerosene are common fuels; hydrogen produces only water when burned, making it the cleanest—candidates may overlook this due to familiarity with conventional fuels.
elimination_hint: Hydrogen combustion produces only water vapor—zero carbon emissions, zero particulate matter; select C.
factual_hooks: Hydrogen fuel: H2 + O2 → H2O only; no CO2, no SOx, no particulates
Green hydrogen produced via renewable electrolysis is fully clean
Diesel and kerosene emit CO2, SOx, NOx, PM2.5
contrast_with: Hydrogen (cleanest) vs CNG (cleaner than diesel but still emits CO2)
contemporary_relevance: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023) targets 5 MT green hydrogen by 2030.

subject: History
trap_factor: Aryabhata is commonly credited with zero; but historical evidence attributes the invention of zero (as a number/placeholder) to an unknown Indian mathematician—Aryabhata used it but didn't invent it.
elimination_hint: The concept of zero was developed by an unknown Indian mathematician; Aryabhata used it but historical records don't conclusively attribute invention to any named individual—select D.
factual_hooks: The inventor of zero is not definitively known—credited to 'an unknown Indian'
Earliest documented use of zero as a number: Bakhshali Manuscript (~3rd-4th century CE)
Aryabhata (5th century CE) used zero in his mathematical work but didn't claim to invent it
contrast_with: Aryabhata (astronomy/math innovator) vs unknown inventor of zero—different contributions

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Civil servant performance matrix should include integrity, empathy, accountability, efficiency, and commitment to constitutional values
ARC II recommended multi-dimensional performance evaluation for civil servants
contrast_with: Technical competence (skills) vs ethical traits (values)—both needed for civil service excellence
mains_intro: Evaluating a civil servant's performance requires a matrix that captures not just technical output but the ethical foundation underlying their conduct.
mains_body_points: Integrity: adherence to rules and resistance to corruption—measurable through audit findings and whistleblower complaints
Empathy: sensitivity to public needs, especially marginalized groups—assessed through public feedback
Accountability: willingness to own decisions and be transparent—indicated by RTI responses, audit compliance
Impartiality: non-discriminatory service delivery—complaint ratios across demographic groups
Commitment to constitutional values: respect for rights and democratic norms—peer review and judicial observations
mains_conclusion: A performance matrix combining these five ethical traits with technical outcomes would ensure civil servants are evaluated holistically as public trustees rather than mere bureaucrats.
contemporary_relevance: DARPG's Mission Karmayogi focuses on capacity and competency building including ethical dimensions.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: AB blood group has both A and B antigens but NO antibodies against these; candidates confuse 'lacks antigens' with 'lacks antibodies'.
elimination_hint: AB blood group has no antibodies in plasma (neither anti-A nor anti-B), so it can receive any blood type without agglutination—select B.
factual_hooks: AB blood group: has A and B antigens on RBCs, but NO antibodies in plasma
Universal recipient because absent antibodies means no rejection of donor blood
O negative is universal donor (no antigens on RBCs)
contrast_with: AB (universal recipient, lacks antibodies) vs O negative (universal donor, lacks antigens)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Chorion and allantois also have protective functions; amnion specifically protects against desiccation by surrounding the embryo in amniotic fluid.
elimination_hint: Amnion = innermost fetal membrane forming amniotic sac filled with amniotic fluid, preventing desiccation; select A.
factual_hooks: Amnion is the innermost fetal membrane producing amniotic fluid that prevents desiccation
Chorion is the outermost membrane involved in gas exchange
Allantois is involved in waste storage and gas exchange
contrast_with: Amnion (desiccation protection) vs Chorion (outermost, gas exchange)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Streptomycin treats TB; Rifampicin treats both TB and leprosy—candidates may choose Streptomycin thinking it's the dual-action drug.
elimination_hint: Rifampicin is used in both TB (DOTS regimen) and multibacillary leprosy (MDT regimen); select D.
factual_hooks: Rifampicin: key drug in both TB treatment (DOTS) and leprosy treatment (MDT)
Streptomycin: used only for TB, not leprosy
Dapsone: used for leprosy but not TB
contrast_with: Rifampicin (both TB+leprosy) vs Streptomycin (TB only)
contemporary_relevance: India eliminated leprosy as public health problem in 2005; TB elimination target is 2025.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Low temperature seems logical for night-blooming; but the real reason is that desert pollinators (moths, bats) are nocturnal—flowers bloom when pollinators are active.
elimination_hint: Desert plants bloom at night because their pollinators (insects, moths) are active at night to avoid daytime heat; select D.
factual_hooks: Desert plants bloom at night to attract nocturnal pollinators (moths, bats, night insects)
Pollinator availability, not temperature, drives bloom timing
Many desert flowers also reduce water loss by blooming in cooler nights
contrast_with: Photoperiodism (day-length trigger) vs pollinator activity (biological trigger) for blooming

subject: Environment
trap_factor: King Cobra is also the largest venomous snake; the nesting behavior is a distinct and lesser-known fact that candidates may not associate with it.
elimination_hint: King Cobra (Ophiophagus hannah) is the only snake species known to build nests for its eggs; select B.
factual_hooks: King Cobra builds a nest of leaves and guards eggs until hatching
Only snake species with nest-building behavior
King Cobra is also the world's longest venomous snake
contrast_with: King Cobra (nest builder) vs other snakes (lay eggs without nesting)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students might omit oxygen or nitrogen; all four (C, H, O, N) are present in all proteins—sulfur is present in some but not all.
elimination_hint: All proteins contain carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen as the backbone of amino acids; select D (all four).
factual_hooks: Proteins are polymers of amino acids; all amino acids contain C, H, O, and N
Sulfur present in methionine and cysteine but not all amino acids
General amino acid formula: NH2-CHR-COOH (contains C, H, O, N)
contrast_with: Elements in all proteins (C, H, O, N) vs elements in some proteins (S, Fe, Zn)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Insulin contains zinc; adrenaline contains no iodine; only thyroxine contains iodine as a structural component.
elimination_hint: Thyroxine (T4) contains four iodine atoms per molecule; it is the only major hormone with iodine in its structure; select A.
factual_hooks: Thyroxine (T4) has 4 iodine atoms; Triiodothyronine (T3) has 3 iodine atoms
Iodine deficiency causes hypothyroidism and goiter
Thyroid gland is the only organ that concentrates iodine
contrast_with: Thyroxine (iodine-containing) vs insulin (zinc-containing) hormones

subject: Governance
trap_factor: G.N. Lewis and Walter Heitler also worked at the molecular level; but Linus Pauling uniquely bridged physics, chemistry, and biology AND promoted Vitamin C.
elimination_hint: Linus Pauling: Nobel laureate (Chemistry 1954, Peace 1962), known for molecular biology, protein structure, and advocating Vitamin C; select B.
factual_hooks: Linus Carl Pauling: only person to win two unshared Nobel Prizes
Described chemical bonding nature and protein alpha-helix structure
Controversial advocacy of Vitamin C for preventing cancer and common cold
contrast_with: Pauling (chemistry+peace) vs Marie Curie (physics+chemistry)—both rare multiple Nobel winners

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Corporate espionage involves theft of confidential business information
Whistleblowing vs complicity in unethical acts is a central ethical dilemma
contrast_with: Short-term personal gain vs long-term ethical integrity and organizational trust
mains_intro: Prabhat's situation presents a classic ethical dilemma where personal financial vulnerability creates pressure to compromise professional integrity and participate in corporate espionage.
mains_body_points: Ethical issues: (a) Corporate espionage—Subhash Verma's offer involves theft of competitor's bid documents, a clear legal and ethical violation; (b) Conflict of interest—Prabhat's personal financial crisis impairs objective judgment; (c) Complicity—accepting the offer makes Prabhat party to unethical and illegal acts; (d) Breach of trust—undermines fair competition and business ethics norms

Options available: (i) Accept offer—resolves financial crisis but involves legal risk, ethical compromise, and possible termination/prosecution; (ii) Reject and report to management—upholds integrity but may not secure his position; (iii) Reject and report to authorities—most ethical but risks personal consequences; (iv) Delay/stall—avoids immediate decision but doesn't resolve the dilemma

Most appropriate: Reject Subhash Verma's offer immediately. Report the attempt to management and if necessary to relevant authorities. Separately address financial issues through legitimate channels (bank negotiation, cost-cutting, alternative employment)
mains_conclusion: An ethical civil servant or professional must refuse to compromise integrity regardless of personal pressures—short-term gains from unethical acts invariably lead to long-term personal and organizational damage.
contemporary_relevance: Corporate espionage and conflict of interest in public procurement are live governance and ethics concerns in India.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: The passage hints at herbicides used as defoliants; candidates may choose the complex DDT option; but Agent Orange's toxic component was dioxin (TCDD contamination).
elimination_hint: Agent Orange was a defoliant (herbicide mixture) contaminated with dioxin (TCDD); select D—dioxin used as defoliants.
factual_hooks: Agent Orange: mixture of herbicides (2,4-D and 2,4,5-T) contaminated with dioxin (TCDD)
Used as defoliant in Vietnam War to destroy jungle cover
Dioxin causes cancer, birth defects, and long-term health impacts
contrast_with: Agent Orange (dioxin-contaminated herbicide) vs DDT (insecticide)—different chemical classes

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The description 'begins as a single cell' could suggest HIV/AIDS; but 'merciless disease that claims millions of lives' with 'turning point in therapies' fits cancer precisely.
elimination_hint: The passage describes cancer—begins as a single abnormal cell, claims millions, therapies advancing rapidly; select A.
factual_hooks: Cancer originates from a single mutant cell undergoing uncontrolled division
Leading cause of mortality globally alongside cardiovascular disease
Immunotherapy and targeted therapy represent modern 'turning points' in cancer treatment
contrast_with: Cancer (single cell → tumor) vs AIDS (viral infection)—both kill millions but different pathology

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Students may confuse yards and metres; 1 yard = 0.9144 metres, so 100 yards = 91.44 m; difference = 100 - 91.44 = 8.56 m.
elimination_hint: 100 metres − 100 yards (91.44 m) = 8.56 m; select B.
factual_hooks: 1 yard = 0.9144 metres
100 yards = 91.44 metres
Difference: 100 m − 91.44 m = 8.56 m

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The complex visibility constraints require careful mapping; candidates often misplace cap positions when working backwards from what each person can see.
elimination_hint: Working backward: G (last) can't see orange → orange is G's cap; E wears indigo → F is between E and G; from visibility clues, F wears red; select C.
factual_hooks: Queue order: A B C D E F G (front to back)
Each sees caps of those ahead; G sees all except own cap's colour
E wears indigo; G can't see orange → orange on G; deduce F wears red
